questions.question: Which court in Ireland has jurisdiction to try all summary (minor) criminal offences, and what constitutional provision governs this jurisdiction?

options.A: The Circuit Court — which has unlimited jurisdiction to try all criminal offences
options.B: The District Court — whose criminal jurisdiction to try summary (minor) offences without a jury is established under Article 34 of the Constitution and s.5(1) of the Courts (Establishment and Constitution) Act 1961; the District Court is the court of first instance for the vast majority of criminal prosecutions in Ireland
options.C: The High Court — which has original jurisdiction to try all minor and serious offences
options.D: The Special Criminal Court — which tries both minor and serious offences without a jury

questions.explanation: The District Court has jurisdiction to try summary (minor) criminal offences — the category of offences tried without a jury. The constitutional basis is Article 34 of the Constitution (establishing the court structure) and s.5(1) of the Courts (Establishment and Constitution) Act 1961, which established the District Court with its summary jurisdiction. The constitutional basis for trying minor offences without a jury is Article 38.2, which provides that minor offences may be tried by courts of summary jurisdiction. The District Court is the court of first instance for the overwhelming majority of criminal prosecutions in Ireland — approximately 95% of all criminal cases are dealt with there. Judges sit alone (no jury) in the District Court. Option A incorrectly names the Circuit Court. Option C incorrectly names the High Court. Option D incorrectly identifies the Special Criminal Court.

questions.question: What is the criminal jurisdiction of the Circuit Court, and what legislative basis establishes it?

options.A: The Circuit Court has no criminal jurisdiction — it hears only civil appeals from the District Court
options.B: Under s.4 of the Courts (Establishment and Constitution) Act 1961, the Circuit Court has jurisdiction to try indictable offences — serious offences tried before a judge and jury; it also hears criminal appeals from the District Court; the Circuit Court is the ordinary court for the trial of indictable offences, apart from the most serious offences which are reserved for the Central Criminal Court
options.C: The Circuit Court has jurisdiction to try all criminal offences including murder, rape, and treason — it is the court of general criminal jurisdiction
options.D: The Circuit Court's criminal jurisdiction is limited to offences carrying a maximum sentence of 5 years or less

questions.explanation: Section 4 of the Courts (Establishment and Constitution) Act 1961 establishes the Circuit Court's criminal jurisdiction. The Circuit Court is the ordinary court for the trial of indictable offences — serious criminal offences tried before a judge sitting with a jury. The judge rules on questions of law; the jury determines questions of fact. The Circuit Court also hears appeals from the District Court on points of law and appeals against conviction and sentence. However, the Circuit Court does not have jurisdiction over the most serious indictable offences (murder, rape, aggravated sexual assault, treason, piracy) — these are reserved for the Central Criminal Court (High Court on criminal side). Option A incorrectly denies any criminal jurisdiction. Option C incorrectly extends jurisdiction to murder and other serious reserved offences. Option D incorrectly limits the jurisdiction to a 5-year maximum.

questions.question: Section 29 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1967 reserves certain categories of serious offences for exclusive trial before the Central Criminal Court. What are these offences?

options.A: All offences carrying a maximum sentence of 10 years or more are reserved for the Central Criminal Court
options.B: Under s.29 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1967, the following offences are reserved for exclusive trial in the Central Criminal Court: murder; attempted murder; conspiracy to murder; rape; aggravated sexual assault; treason; and piracy; these offences are of such gravity that the legislature determined they should only be tried by the highest ordinary criminal court
options.C: The Central Criminal Court has exclusive jurisdiction over all offences committed in Dublin — it is a geographically limited court
options.D: The Central Criminal Court has exclusive jurisdiction only over murder and rape — all other serious offences can be tried in either the Circuit Court or Central Criminal Court

questions.explanation: Section 29 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1967 provides that certain categories of the most serious criminal offences must be tried exclusively in the Central Criminal Court (which is the High Court exercising its criminal jurisdiction under Art 34.3.1 of the Constitution). The reserved offences include: murder; attempted murder; conspiracy to murder; rape; aggravated sexual assault; treason; and piracy. The reservation of these offences to the Central Criminal Court reflects their unique gravity — they are the most serious offences in the criminal calendar and are committed for trial to the highest ordinary court. FE-1 candidates must know the list of reserved offences — this is a common examination point. Option A incorrectly makes all 10-year+ offences reserved. Option C incorrectly makes jurisdiction geographic. Option D understates the full list of reserved offences.

questions.question: The Central Criminal Court is constitutionally grounded under Article 34.3.1 of the Constitution. What does this provision establish?

options.A: Article 34.3.1 establishes the District Court as the primary court of criminal jurisdiction
options.B: Article 34.3.1 establishes that the High Court shall have full original jurisdiction in and power to determine all matters and questions whether of law or fact, civil or criminal — it is on this basis that the High Court exercises criminal jurisdiction as the Central Criminal Court; the Central Criminal Court is thus the High Court sitting in its criminal capacity, with exclusive jurisdiction over the most serious reserved indictable offences
options.C: Article 34.3.1 establishes the Special Criminal Court as a permanent feature of the constitutional order
options.D: Article 34.3.1 restricts the High Court to civil matters only — the constitution requires a separate criminal court system

questions.explanation: Article 34.3.1 of the Constitution provides that the High Court shall have 'full original jurisdiction in and power to determine all matters and questions whether of law or fact, civil or criminal.' This full original jurisdiction is the constitutional basis for the Central Criminal Court — the High Court sitting in its criminal capacity. When the High Court exercises its criminal jurisdiction, it does so as the Central Criminal Court. The Central Criminal Court has exclusive jurisdiction over the reserved offences listed in s.29 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1967. Article 34.3.1 is the constitutional anchor for this jurisdiction. FE-1 candidates should know both the constitutional basis (Art 34.3.1) and the statutory basis (s.29 CPA 1967) for the Central Criminal Court. Option A incorrectly identifies the District Court. Option C incorrectly identifies the Special Criminal Court. Option D incorrectly restricts the High Court to civil matters.

questions.question: The Special Criminal Court is established under Article 38.3.1 of the Constitution. In what circumstances can it exercise jurisdiction, and what was confirmed in Kavanagh v Ireland [1996]?

options.A: The Special Criminal Court can only exercise jurisdiction for offences expressly listed in the Constitution
options.B: Article 38.3.1 permits the establishment of special courts for offences in cases where the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice and the preservation of public peace and order; Kavanagh v Ireland [1996] confirmed that the Government's determination that the ordinary courts are inadequate is not justiciable — courts will not review the government's decision to activate special court jurisdiction in any particular case
options.C: Kavanagh v Ireland [1996] struck down the Special Criminal Court as unconstitutional — the court has been re-established on a different basis
options.D: Kavanagh v Ireland [1996] held that accused persons have the right to have the basis for referral to the Special Criminal Court reviewed by the High Court on the merits

questions.explanation: Article 38.3.1 permits the establishment of special courts for the trial of offences 'in cases where it may be determined in accordance with such law that the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice, and the preservation of public peace and order.' In Kavanagh v Ireland [1996], the Supreme Court confirmed that the government's determination that the ordinary courts are inadequate — which triggers the referral of cases to the Special Criminal Court — is not justiciable; the courts will not review the merits of this determination. This significantly limits the ability of accused persons to challenge their referral to the Special Criminal Court. The Special Criminal Court sits without a jury, with three judges. More recently, DPP v Dowdall and Hutch [2022] confirmed the court's jurisdiction over a major criminal trial. Option A incorrectly limits jurisdiction to Constitutional offences only. Option C incorrectly states the court was struck down. Option D is the opposite of what Kavanagh actually held.

questions.question: In DPP v Dowdall and Hutch [2022] (the Regency Hotel murder trial), the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction was extensively tested. What is the significance of this case for understanding the Special Criminal Court?

options.A: The case resulted in the abolition of the Special Criminal Court — the government disbanded the court following criticism of the proceedings
options.B: DPP v Dowdall and Hutch [2022] was a major, high-profile trial in the Special Criminal Court — one of the longest and most complex trials ever conducted in Ireland; the case confirmed and demonstrated the Special Criminal Court's capacity to manage complex, lengthy serious criminal trials; it illustrated how the three-judge non-jury court functions in practice for cases where jury tampering is a concern
options.C: The case established that the Special Criminal Court cannot try murder charges — murder must be tried by the Central Criminal Court
options.D: The case held that accused persons referred to the Special Criminal Court are entitled to a trial by jury under Art 38.5 of the Constitution

questions.explanation: DPP v Dowdall and Hutch [2022] (the Regency Hotel murder trial) was one of the most significant criminal trials in recent Irish legal history. The case arose from the murder of David Byrne at the Regency Hotel in Dublin in February 2016 during a boxing weigh-in — an incident connected to the Kinahan-Hutch feud. The trial before the Special Criminal Court was of enormous length and complexity. Its significance includes: (1) confirming the Special Criminal Court's practical capacity to manage complex, high-profile criminal trials; (2) demonstrating the court's operation in a case where jury interference was a genuine concern; and (3) providing a recent, high-profile example of the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction in action. Note: Gerry Hutch was ultimately acquitted. Option A incorrectly states the court was abolished. Option C incorrectly states the Special Criminal Court cannot try murder — it can, unlike the Central Criminal Court which has exclusive original jurisdiction, both can try murder. Option D is the opposite of how the Special Criminal Court functions.

questions.question: Section 2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1951 provides for the 'hybrid' or 'either-way' category of offence. What does this provision enable?

options.A: Section 2 enables the accused to elect between trial by jury and trial without jury for any criminal offence
options.B: Section 2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1951 provides that certain indictable offences (those specified in the Schedule) may, in certain circumstances, be tried summarily in the District Court — if the DPP consents to summary trial, the District Court judge is satisfied it is minor, and the accused consents; this creates a 'hybrid' category that can be tried either way depending on these conditions
options.C: Section 2 enables the DPP to unilaterally decide that any offence will be tried summarily — the accused and court have no role in this decision
options.D: Section 2 provides that all indictable offences must be tried on indictment — it reinforces the right to jury trial and prevents summary disposal of serious offences

questions.explanation: Section 2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1951 creates the mechanism for 'hybrid' or 'either-way' offences in Irish law. Certain indictable offences listed in the Schedule to the Act may be tried summarily in the District Court if three conditions are met: (1) the DPP (or Director of Public Prosecutions) consents to summary trial; (2) the District Court judge is satisfied that the offence is minor and the facts are suitable for summary disposal; and (3) the accused consents to summary trial. If any of these conditions is absent, the case proceeds by indictment. The hybrid category is a practical solution allowing less serious instances of technically indictable offences to be disposed of efficiently in the District Court. This is covered further in Module 13 Lesson 2 on classification. Option A incorrectly makes election available for any offence without the three conditions. Option C incorrectly removes the court and accused from the process. Option D misstates the effect of s.2.

questions.question: In Kevin Lunney [2021] IESC, the Supreme Court considered an important question about the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction. What was the key issue and the court's conclusion?

options.A: The Supreme Court held that the Special Criminal Court had no jurisdiction to try offences involving non-State actors — its jurisdiction is limited to State subversion cases
options.B: Kevin Lunney [2021] IESC concerned the question of whether the accused could be tried in the Special Criminal Court for serious offences not falling within the traditional State subversion category — the Supreme Court confirmed that the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction can extend to serious organised crime cases where the ordinary courts are inadequate, not just to traditional State subversion offences
options.C: The Supreme Court struck down the procedure by which cases are referred to the Special Criminal Court as unconstitutional
options.D: The Supreme Court held that jury trial is required for all murder charges — the Special Criminal Court cannot try murder regardless of the circumstances

questions.explanation: Kevin Lunney [2021] IESC is a significant Supreme Court decision on the scope of the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction. The case arose from the serious assault and kidnapping of Kevin Lunney, a director of Quinn Industrial Holdings, in County Fermanagh in 2019. The Supreme Court considered whether the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction extended beyond traditional State subversion cases to serious organised crime cases. The court confirmed that the Special Criminal Court can be used for cases involving serious organised crime where the ordinary courts are determined to be inadequate — the jurisdiction is not limited to traditional subversion offences under the OATSA 1939. This is an important modern development confirming the court's broader potential reach. Options A, C, and D each misstate the holding of the case — the jurisdiction was confirmed, not restricted or struck down.

questions.question: Donna is charged with rape — one of the offences reserved for the Central Criminal Court under s.29 CPA 1967. The DPP applies to have the case transferred to the Special Criminal Court on the basis that the ordinary courts are inadequate. What court will try the case?

options.A: The Circuit Court — which has concurrent jurisdiction with the Central Criminal Court for rape cases
options.B: The Special Criminal Court — when the DPP determines that the ordinary courts are inadequate and applies to have a reserved offence tried in the Special Criminal Court, this is permitted under Art 38.3.1 of the Constitution; the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction is not precluded by the reservation of rape to the Central Criminal Court; the Special Criminal Court can try any offence, including those ordinarily reserved for the Central Criminal Court, when the ordinary courts are determined to be inadequate
options.C: The Central Criminal Court — s.29 CPA 1967 creates an absolute and exclusive reservation that cannot be displaced even by application to the Special Criminal Court
options.D: The District Court — the reservation in s.29 applies only to Circuit Court jurisdiction; the District Court and Special Criminal Court are not subject to it

questions.explanation: This scenario tests the interaction between the s.29 CPA 1967 reservation and the Special Criminal Court's constitutional jurisdiction. The answer is the Special Criminal Court. The s.29 CPA 1967 reservation applies to the ordinary courts — it identifies which ordinary court (Central Criminal Court rather than Circuit Court) must try reserved offences. However, the Special Criminal Court is not an ordinary court — it is a special court established under Art 38.3.1 of the Constitution. The constitutional basis for the Special Criminal Court is separate from and operates alongside the ordinary court system. The DPP can refer any case to the Special Criminal Court where the ordinary courts are determined to be inadequate — this power is not restricted by the s.29 reservation, which operates within the ordinary court framework. Option A incorrectly identifies the Circuit Court. Option C incorrectly treats the s.29 reservation as absolute against the Special Criminal Court. Option D is incorrect.
